title: Add --replace flag to podman secret create
body:
Users may want to update the secret used within containers, without destroying the secret and recreating it.

Partial fix for https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/18667

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
Podman secret create now has a --replace option, which allows you to modify secrets without replacing containers.
```

comments.body:
[APPROVALNOTIFIER] This PR is **APPROVED** This pull-request has been approved by: *rhatdan* The full list of commands accepted by this bot can be found [here](https://go.k8s.io/bot-commands?repo=containers%2Fpodman). The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [rhatdan] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment
@ashley-cui PTAL

I think the way update in common is written is that the secret ID will not change on an update, and `podman secret create --replace` implies that the secret is a new secret, with a new ID, but it replaces the old secret with the name. Maybe `podman secret create --update`? Or is this different enough to be another command? 

Code otherwise LGTM, open to suggestions on naming, not opposed anything ATM
I went with replace since we use this one for `podman container create --replace`
@ashley-cui Would it make sense to change the ID, or would this break existing containers that use the secret?

That would break containers that use the secret. 

I believe `container create --replace` does change the container ID.

Well, kind of breaks. If I recall correctly the once a container is created with a secret, the secret is never updated in the container, even if the secret is deleted or changed. It would only break if a user re-ran the container when specifying a secret using its ID.
So you are saying the secret is embedded in the container, restarting the container will not get the new secret?
Yes, that's correct. I believe that's why we didn't implement updating secrets in the first pass of secrets because it wouldn't change the secret inside created containers in the first place.
@kriansa is this what you intended? IE Change the password in existing containers or are you thinking just for newly created containers?

I'm fine with how it's been implemented given the complexity of only doing it in newly created containers. In many cases, applications are not prepared to hot reload secrets anyway and it's common to redeploy (i.e. recreate) them just to ensure they load up the correct values on initialization. See for example [reloader], which does exactly that on Kubernetes.

For those relying on stateful containers, we might want to show a warning message when you attempt to replace a secret currently in use. WDYT?

[reloader]: https://github.com/stakater/Reloader

> For those relying on stateful containers, we might want to show a warning message when you attempt to replace a secret currently in use. WDYT?

Great question!  Currently, `podman secret rm` does not check whether a secret's in use, so I think `--replace` should just follow `rm`.

/lgtm
/hold

Thanks! Sorry for the fuss on naming the flag, I just want to make sure we're consistent in our flags between our commands
New changes are detected. LGTM label has been removed.

We are sticking with --replace, not --update.  If we want to add an update mechanism I think we should add
`podman secret update ` command and then have it affect all containers. (Optionally)

> We are sticking with --replace, not --update. If we want to add an update mechanism I think we should add `podman secret update ` command and then have it affect all containers. (Optionally)

`--replace` should then create an entirely new secret (and ID) to be consistent with other commands.
Thats the plan
Thanks!
I found a bug in Common,  Sadly need to do another vendor..
@vrothberg Something strange is going on with containers/common. The older released version keeps getting sucked in unless I do a `go mod edit --replace`

I think this is the PR fix you made in common to address this: https://github.com/containers/common/pull/1541  If so, it went into the main branch and not the v0.55 branch.  I think you need a cherry-pick there.

> I think this is the PR fix you made in common to address this: [containers/common#1541](https://github.com/containers/common/pull/1541) If so, it went into the main branch and not the v0.55 branch. I think you need a cherry-pick there.

This is for a new feature that is not part of Podman 4.6.  I don't think it needs backporting to the v0.55 branch.
